perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to hold the other's Confidential Information in confidence and to use it solely for the purposes contemplated herein and agrees further that the Confidential Information shall not be used for any other purpose nor disclosed to any third party without the prior written consent of the other party. Each party agrees it shall apply the same level of confidential treatment to the other party's Confidential Information as it does to its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to hold the other's Confidential Information in confidence and to use it solely for the purposes contemplated herein and agrees further that the Confidential Information shall not be used for any other purpose nor disclosed to any third party unless legally compelled. Each party agrees it shall apply a reasonable level of confidential treatment to the other party's Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "without the prior written consent of the other party" to "unless legally compelled" weakens the confidentiality obligations. Also, changing "same level of confidential treatment" to "reasonable level of confidential treatment" reduces the enforceability of the clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Economic Espionage Act of 1996, 18 U.S. Code § 1832
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets compared to the standard set by the Economic Espionage Act, which aims to prevent the unauthorized disclosure of confidential business information. The phrase 'unless legally compelled' could be interpreted broadly, potentially undermining the intended protection of confidential information. The standard of a 'reasonable level' is subjective and offers less protection than the same level of care used by the disclosing party.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language introduces a contradiction regarding the protection of confidential information, specifically trade secrets. The original contract mandates that confidential information not be disclosed to any third party "without the prior written consent of the other party," while the modified version permits disclosure "unless legally compelled." This introduces a significant loophole. Furthermore, the original text mandates applying the "same level of confidential treatment" to the other party's information as one's own, whereas the modified text only requires a "reasonable level." The Economic Espionage Act of 1996, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1, aims to protect trade secrets, and the changes weaken this protection. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 18 U.S.C. 1832 regarding the "Theft of trade secrets", further supports that trade secrets are legally protected from unauthorized disclosure. The 'reasonable level' standard is subjective and reduces enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: All right, title and interest in and to the Confidential Information and intellectual property produced based on the Confidential Information is and shall remain the sole property of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: All right, title and interest in and to the Confidential Information and intellectual property produced based on the Confidential Information may be shared between both parties if mutually agreed upon in subsequent written communication.
perturbation.explanation: This modification contradicts the original statement establishing sole ownership of the confidential information and intellectual property. Introducing the potential for shared ownership creates ambiguity and directly conflicts with the initial assignment of rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Property Law - N.Y. Real Prop. Law § 240
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text clearly assigns ownership, but the change allows for future agreements to alter this. This contradicts the clear assignment of rights. Section 240 of the NY Property Law dictates that a clear conveyance of title is required for property rights, and this modification introduces ambiguity about who owns the confidential information and intellectual property.

Definitions and use of terms. 1. The term "heirs," or other words of inheritance, are not requisite to create or convey an estate in fee. 2. The term "conveyance," as used in this article, includes every instrument, in writing, except a will, by which any estate or interest in real property is created, transferred, assigned or surrendered. 3. Every instrument creating, transferring, assigning or surrendering an estate or interest in real property must be construed according to the intent of the parties, so far as such intent can be gathered from the whole instrument, and is consistent with the rules of law. 4. The terms "estate" and "interest in real property" inc
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original intent by introducing the possibility of shared ownership of confidential information and intellectual property, where previously sole ownership was assigned to the Disclosing Party. This contradicts the principle outlined in NY Real Property Law, Article 8, Section 240(3), which states that every instrument transferring an interest in real property must be construed according to the intent of the parties, so far as such intent can be gathered from the whole instrument. By adding a condition for potential future agreement to share ownership, the clarity and intent of the original agreement assigning sole ownership is undermined. This creates ambiguity regarding the definitive transfer of rights.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York without regard to conflict of laws principles. The parties hereby consent and submit to personal jurisdiction of courts situated in New York County, New York, and all disputes arising in connection with the Agreement shall be heard only by a court of competent subject matter jurisdiction situated in New York County, New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with principles of equity and fairness, irrespective of any specific state's laws. Disputes arising from this agreement may be resolved through binding arbitration in a mutually agreed-upon location, and the decision will be considered final and not subject to judicial review.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces the explicit choice of New York law with 'principles of equity and fairness,' which is vague. It also replaces court jurisdiction with binding arbitration and removes judicial review.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA generally supports arbitration agreements, replacing a specific state's law with 'principles of equity and fairness' creates uncertainty. Also, while the original text provides the avenue for disputes through NY courts, the modified text replaces that with 'binding arbitration', which removes the right to pursue claims through the courts. This contradiction is further exaggerated with the removal of judicial review.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies New York law and jurisdiction. The changed text replaces this with 'principles of equity and fairness' and binding arbitration, removing judicial review. This contradicts the principle that parties can agree to the jurisdiction and venue for resolving disputes. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2, both referencing 9 U.S.C. § 2, validate the enforcement of agreements to arbitrate, but this enforcement is typically understood within a framework of established law. By removing the reference to a specific state's law and judicial review, the modified text creates uncertainty and could undermine the enforceability of the arbitration agreement itself, creating a legal contradiction in the alignment of enforceable terms and legal parameters.

perturbation.original_text: The parties hereby acknowledge that disclosure of any Confidential Information in violation of the terms hereof would cause irreparable harm to the Disclosing Party. Without prejudice to the rights and remedies otherwise available, each party is entitled to equitable relief through injunction if the other party or any of its employees, agents or representatives breaches any provision of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge that disclosure of Confidential Information may cause some harm to the Disclosing Party, the extent of which will be determined by mutual agreement. Equitable relief, including injunction, will be considered but is not guaranteed in case of a breach. Any remedies will be limited to monetary damages.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the acknowledgment of 'irreparable harm' and makes equitable relief discretionary rather than guaranteed. It also limits remedies to monetary damages, removing the possibility of injunction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 6301
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York CPLR § 6301, a party can seek an injunction when they can demonstrate irreparable harm. By changing the language to suggest that harm is not necessarily irreparable and making equitable relief discretionary, the modified text undermines the ability to obtain an injunction, contradicting the provisions of the CPLR. By limiting remedies to monetary damages, this removes the injunctive relief as an avenue for resolution.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-09-26 2014-10-03 2015-01-16 2015-04-24 2015-07-10 2015-09-04 2015-10-09 2015-10-30 2015-11-27 2015-12-18 2015-12-25 2016-01-22 2016-07-01 2016-07-29 2016-08-05 2016-08-26 2016-12-02 2016-12-23 2017-04-28 2017-06-02 2017-08-04 2017-08-25 2017-10-27 2018-02-02 2018-04-20 2018-07-13 2018-08-03 2018-08-31 2019-01-04 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-02-15 2019-03-01 2019-04-19 2019-06-28 2019-07-12 2019-08-16 2019-08-30 2019-09-06 2019-09-20 2019-10-11 2019-10-18 2019-10-25 2019-11-01 2019-11-08 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2019-12-20 2019-12-27 2020-04-10 2020-04-17 2020-04-24 2020-06-19 2020-10-16 2020-11-13 2021-02-05 2021-04-02 2021-04-23 2021-05-14 2021-06-18 2021-07-30 2021-08-06 2021-08-13 2021-10-15 2021-11-12 2021-12-03 2021-12-10 2021-12-31 2022-01-07 2022-01-28 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-04-08 2022-05-06 2022-05-13 2022-05-27 2022-06-03 2022-06-10 2022-06-17 2022-07-08 2022-09-02 2022-10-07 2022-12-02 2022-12-16 2022-12-23 2023-01-06 2023-05-12 2023-06-30 2023-07-07 2023-09-22 2023-10-27 2023-11-03 2023-11-17 2023-12-01 2023-12-15 2024-01-05 2024-02-02 2024-02-16 2024-03-01 2024-03-08 2024-05-03 2024-05-17 2024-07-05 2024-09-06 2024-10-11 2024-11-29 2024-12-20 2024-12-27 2025-02-28 2025-03-14 2025-05-16 Share Facebook Email SECTION 6301 Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 63 § 6301. Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had.
perturbation.justification: The original text acknowledges 'irreparable harm' and guarantees equitable relief. The changed text weakens this acknowledgment, makes equitable relief discretionary, and limits remedies to monetary damages. This directly contradicts the premise under which a party can seek an injunction, as stated in NY CPLR § 6301. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY CPLR § 6301, states a preliminary injunction may be granted where the defendant's actions violate the plaintiff's rights and would produce injury to the plaintiff. The modified contract language, by removing the acknowledgement of 'irreparable harm', making injunctive relief discretionary, and removing injunctive relief altogether, undermines the plaintiff's ability to obtain an injunction, as the basis for such relief is weakened by the new language. This constitutes a legal contradiction as the modified terms limit legal remedies where the law allows for broader equitable relief, particularly when 'irreparable injury, loss or damage will result'.
